The conversation, recorded before the Defense Department’s conflict with Anthropic intensified, also revealed long-held resentment about his and Travis Kalanick’s 2017 departures from Uber. Michael admitted he remains deeply bitter about being forced out during the fallout from investigations into Uber’s workplace culture, even though he wasn’t personally implicated. He blames major investors, including Benchmark, for prioritizing short-term gains over Uber’s long-term vision of autonomous driving. The pair’s ouster, he claimed, derailed Uber’s leadership in self-driving technology, a setback he believes continues to haunt the company.
Michael al

item.title: Los Angeles Jury Orders Bill Cosby to Pay $59.25 Million in 1972 Sexual Assault Civil Case
item.description:
A civil jury in Los Angeles has found comedian and actor Bill Cosby liable for a 1972 sexual assault against Donna Motsinger, now 84 years old. The jury awarded Motsinger a total of $59.25 million in damages, including $19.25 million in compensatory damages and $40 million in punitive damages. Motsinger filed the lawsuit in 2023, alleging that Cosby drugged and raped her after inviting her to a show and providing her with wine that left her incapacitated. According to court filings, she recalled losing consciousness and later waking up partially undressed, concluding she had been assaulted.
The lawsuit also accused Cosby of engaging in a pattern of sexual misconduct involving multiple women over several decades, leveraging his fame and influence. Cosby did not testify during the trial. He has consistently denied all allegations of sexual assault, despite being accused by at least 60 women over the years.
Cosby was previously convicted of sexual assault in 2018 in a separate case, but

item.title: Understanding the Psychological Roots of Silence in Alleged Abuse by Cesar Chavez
item.description:
The Conversation article explores why multiple women who accused Mexican American civil rights leader Cesar Chavez of sexual abuse waited decades before speaking publicly. The allegations, uncovered by The New York Times after a yearslong investigation, reveal patterns of silence consistent with betrayal trauma — a psychological response that occurs when victims are harmed by people they deeply trust or depend on. The piece explains that victims in such scenarios often suppress or dissociate from their experiences to preserve essential relationships, financial support, or community belonging. These dynamics are intensified when the abuser holds social, cultural, or institutional power.
Clinical psychologist research cited in the article connects this silence to 'betrayal trauma theory,' which shows that disclosure often carries heavy emotional, social, and practical risks. Victims may fear disbelief, blame, or damaging the reputations of institutions and causes they value — a form of

item.title: Seedance 2.0 AI Shows Potential in Film Creation, But Challenges Remain
item.description: The rise of AI-generated video is starting to impact multiple industries, and the film sector is particularly concerned. While most AI clips on social media are superficial or humorous, deepfakes and educational videos for children are raising ethical and misinformation concerns. The latest development, Seedance 2.0, created by TikTok's parent company ByteDance, represents a significant step forward in AI video generation. Trained on billions of posts, it can produce content that balances entertainment with educational elements, as demonstrated in examples like 'Chloe vs History,' where a digital influencer can travel through time. Despite its capabilities, Seedance 2.0 is not yet capable of independently creating truly watchable films, though it does present a disruptive tool for creators. US studios are wary of AI profiting from their copyrighted material and potentially affecting the industry's economic model. The technology is both promising and controversial, highlighting the tens

item.title: Postmenopausal Women Lose Significantly More Weight When Combining Hormone Therapy with Tirzepatide
item.description: A recent Mayo Clinic study has found that postmenopausal women who combined menopausal hormone therapy with tirzepatide, a medication approved for overweight and obesity, lost approximately 35% more weight than those taking the drug alone. The study analyzed 120 adults treated with tirzepatide for at least 12 months, comparing outcomes between those using hormone therapy and those who were not. While the study was observational and not randomized, suggesting that other factors like lifestyle or improved sleep may contribute, researchers noted the results indicate a potential synergy between estrogen and GLP-1-based medications. Estrogen may enhance the appetite-suppressing effects of drugs like tirzepatide, opening new possibilities for more effective and personalized approaches to weight management in postmenopausal women. Future research will focus on confirming these results through randomized clinical trials and exploring additional cardiometabolic benefits. This work highlights th

item.title: Researchers Identify Protein Interaction Driving Alzheimer’s and Develop Compound to Halt It in Mice
item.description: A research team led by Professor Hilmar Bading at Heidelberg University has uncovered a key biological mechanism behind Alzheimer's disease progression. The scientists identified a harmful interaction between two proteins—the NMDA receptor and the TRPM4 ion channel—that, when bound together, form a destructive protein complex. This complex triggers neuronal death and accelerates memory loss. Using a specially developed compound called FP802, the researchers successfully disrupted this toxic interaction in a mouse model of Alzheimer’s. The drug, described as a TwinF Interface Inhibitor, binds to the exact site where the two proteins connect, preventing the formation of the damaging complex. Mice treated with FP802 showed slower disease progression, preserved memory and learning abilities, and reduced brain damage. The treatment also lowered the accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques, a hallmark of Alzheimer’s pathology. Unlike traditional therapies focused solely on amyloid removal, this

item.title: Swish secures $38 million Series B to expand its fast food delivery model in India
item.description:
Bengaluru-based food delivery startup Swish has raised $38 million in a Series B round led by Hara Global and Bain Capital Ventures, with participation from Accel, Stride Ventures, and Alteria Capital. This brings Swish’s total funding to $54 million and values the company at $139 million post-money, more than double its valuation from a year ago. Founded in 2024, Swish operates a 10-minute fresh food delivery service targeting dense urban areas of Bengaluru. The startup owns its entire operation—kitchens, supply chain, and delivery fleet—allowing for tighter cost control and faster order fulfillment compared to aggregators like Swiggy or Zomato that rely on partner restaurants.
Swish claims to deliver around 20,000 orders per day, up from 5,000 just four months ago, across ten micro-markets. It offers over 200 dishes and beverages, with an average ticket size of ₹200–₹250 (approximately $2–$3). CEO and co-founder Aniket Shah described the business model as a hybrid between restauran

item.title: Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss Secures Illinois 9th District Democratic Nomination
item.description: Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss has won the Democratic primary for Illinois' 9th congressional district, succeeding longtime Rep. Jan Schakowsky after her retirement announcement. The Chicago-area seat, held by Democrats for decades and home to a significant Jewish population, drew national attention due to heavy involvement from pro-Israel groups like AIPAC, which spent over $5 million on ads boosting rival Laura Fine and later attacking Biss and progressive Kat Abughazaleh. Biss, a former state legislator and University of Chicago math professor who ran unsuccessfully for governor in 2018, overcame the crowded field of over a dozen candidates with endorsements from Schakowsky, Sen. Elizabeth Warren, and J Street, a liberal pro-Israel group. He criticized AIPAC's interference in Democratic primaries while aligning with Schakowsky's views on Israel and progressive priorities. His platform emphasized affordable housing investment, Social Security expansion, and banning congressional stock tr

item.title: Typical Lifespan of Rental Property Carpets and Responsibility for Replacement
item.description:
This article explains how long carpets typically last in rental properties and clarifies when landlords or tenants are responsible for replacement. In general, carpets in rentals last between 5 and 7 years, although lower-quality carpets may wear out in as little as 3 to 5 years, while higher-end or commercial-grade options can last up to 10 years with proper care. The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development considers five years to be the standard useful life for rental carpets, which is important when determining depreciation and whether landlords can charge tenants for damage.
Landlords are usually responsible for replacing carpets due to normal wear and tear, such as fading, thinning fibers, or minor marks from everyday use. These conditions are expected over time and cannot be charged to tenants. However, landlords must also ensure the property remains safe and habitable, meaning carpets must be replaced if they become hazardous or develop issues like mold.
Tenants may b

item.title: Air Street Capital Raises $232 Million Fund to Back Early-Stage AI Startups
item.description:
London-based Air Street Capital has announced the launch of its third fund, raising $232 million to invest in early-stage artificial intelligence startups across Europe and North America. The firm, led by Nathan Benaich, is positioning itself as one of the largest solo general partner venture capital firms in Europe with this latest raise. The new fund will primarily target early-stage companies, with initial check sizes ranging from $500,000 to $15 million, while select growth-stage investments may reach up to $25 million.
Air Street has built a strong reputation in the AI investment space, having previously backed notable companies such as Black Forest Labs and ElevenLabs, both considered leading AI unicorns. The firm has also achieved successful exits, including Adept, which was acquired by Amazon, and Graphcore, which was sold to SoftBank. These outcomes highlight the firm’s ability to identify and support high-impact AI ventures.
With the addition of Fund III, Air Street now man

item.title: A Cold War‑Style Numbers Station Resurfaces Over Iran During US–Israel Strikes
item.description: At the start of the US–Israel military campaign against Iran on February 28, 2026, a shortwave radio transmission began airing twice daily on the 7910 kHz frequency, later switching to 7842 kHz after jamming attempts. The broadcast features a man’s voice in Persian repeatedly saying 'Tavajoh!' ('Attention!') before reciting long strings of seemingly random numbers, consistent with a Cold War‑era intelligence technique known as a numbers station. Using radio‑location methods, the monitoring group Priyom has traced the signal to a US military shortwave facility within Böblingen Air Base in southwest Germany, likely linked to the US Army’s 52nd Strategic Signal Battalion. Despite that partial identification, the true operator and intended recipient of the broadcast remain unknown. Security experts and former intelligence officers describe numbers stations as encrypted shortwave channels historically used by spy agencies to send covert instructions to agents, relying on codebooks to decode

item.title: 'The View' Co-Host Sara Haines Supports ICE Assistance at Airports During TSA Challenges
item.description: During a segment on 'The View,' co-host Sara Haines addressed the idea of deploying U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents to assist airport security amid the ongoing Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown. Haines expressed support for the measure, highlighting that TSA agents have been forced to work without pay, causing extensive travel delays. She argued that ICE agents, with extensive federal experience and a large budget, could provide needed manpower at airports, ensuring passenger safety. Guest co-host Abby Huntsman emphasized the public concern over airport security during the shutdown, noting that millions of travelers are affected. Co-host Sunny Hostin disagreed, pointing out that ICE agents receive significantly less training than TSA personnel and are trained for different operational tasks, making them less suitable for airport security. Haines countered that the risk in airports is lower than in ICE operations and emphasized that pragmatic solutions

item.title: Startup R3 Bio Develops Brainless Organ Systems as Ethical Alternative to Animal Testing and Future Human Organ Source
item.description: A Bay Area biotech startup called R3 Bio is developing genetically engineered 'organ sacks'—whole organ systems lacking a brain to eliminate sentience and pain perception—as a replacement for animal testing in drug development. Cofounder Alice Gilman explains that these structures would include typical organs but exclude brain development through stem cell technology and gene editing, starting with mouse models and aiming for monkey versions to test drug toxicity more scalably and ethically. The approach addresses shortages of research monkeys in the US, exacerbated by export bans from China, federal phase-outs of animal experimentation under the Trump administration, and ethical concerns from animal rights groups. R3 Bio draws from the 'three Rs' philosophy (replacement, reduction, refinement) in animal research. Beyond testing, the long-term vision includes creating nonsentient human 'organ sacks' or 'bodyoids' to supply tissues, blood, and organs for transplant patients, tackling th

item.title: Leaked DarkSword Exploit Puts Millions of Older iPhones at Risk
item.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered that a new version of the iPhone hacking tool DarkSword has been publicly leaked on GitHub. This exploit kit targets iPhones and iPads running older versions of iOS, including iOS 18, making hundreds of millions of devices vulnerable. Experts warn that the leaked files are simple HTML and JavaScript scripts, which means even individuals without iOS security knowledge can deploy them quickly to steal sensitive data. The malware can exfiltrate contacts, messages, call history, and passwords stored in iOS keychain to attacker-controlled servers. Researchers from iVerify and Google confirm that the exploits work out of the box and share infrastructure with previous DarkSword versions. Apple has responded by issuing emergency updates for devices unable to run the latest iOS 26 and recommends keeping software up to date, along with using Lockdown Mode to block these attacks. The leak follows the recent discovery of another advanced iPhone hacking too

item.title: Study Reveals Public Misperceptions of Foods' Environmental Impacts
item.description: A new study from the University of Nottingham's School of Psychology shows that people frequently misjudge the environmental impact of everyday foods. Researchers had 168 UK participants sort a wide range of supermarket items into categories based on perceived environmental harm. Many assumed processed foods and animal products were the worst offenders, but they often overlooked the high impact of water-intensive items like nuts and underestimated beef's outsized footprint compared to chicken or other meats. These errors stem from simplistic heuristics, such as animal vs. plant distinctions or processing levels, rather than comprehensive life cycle assessments that factor in emissions, land use, water consumption, and waste from production to disposal. The research, funded by UKRI's Smart Data Research UK and published in the Journal of Cleaner Production, highlights food production's role in greenhouse gas emissions and biodiversity loss. Participants were surprised by scientific impa

item.title: Jessie J Hospitalized for MRI After Hitting Head on Car Roof During China Tour, Rules Out Neck Fracture
item.description: British singer Jessie J, known for hits like 'Price Tag,' experienced a health scare while on her No Secrets tour in China. In a video posted to Instagram, the 37-year-old artist was seen struggling to get into the back of an SUV, where she hit her head hard on the roof. She later shared that she was rushed to the hospital for an MRI scan due to concerns about a possible broken neck. Jessie J admitted she initially thought she had fractured her neck but was relieved to learn it was not broken, though she sustained injuries to her neck and back. Despite the pain and limited head movement, she pushed through with painkillers and continued tour rehearsals and performances. This incident follows a challenging period for the singer, who was diagnosed with early-stage breast cancer in June 2025. She underwent a double mastectomy and was briefly hospitalized again weeks later for a suspected blood clot in her lungs. Jessie J has been open about her recovery journey, including moments of fear

item.title: Jet Fuel Prices Double Amid Iran Conflict, Prompting U.S. and Global Airlines to Cut Flights and Raise Fares
item.description: Jet fuel prices in the United States have more than doubled in recent weeks, rising from approximately $2.17 per gallon to $4.56 per gallon by March 20, 2026, according to the Argus U.S. Jet Fuel Index. This sharp increase is driven by escalating tensions in the Middle East, particularly around the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint that handles about 20 million barrels of oil per day and significant volumes of jet fuel. The Middle East supplies roughly 17% of global jet fuel consumption, making the market highly sensitive to disruptions. Major U.S. airlines are already feeling the impact. United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby announced plans to reduce about 5% of scheduled flights in the near term and suspend select international routes, including those to Israel and Dubai. The carrier warned that sustained high prices could add $11 billion in annual expenses. Delta Air Lines CEO Ed Bastian reported an additional $400 million in fuel costs for March alone, while American Airlines proje

item.title: Study Shows Forests Accumulate Microplastics Primarily Through Atmospheric Deposition
item.description: A recent study by geoscientists at TU Darmstadt, published in Communications Earth & Environment, reveals that forests are accumulating significant amounts of microplastics, primarily through airborne pathways rather than local ground sources. Researchers collected and analyzed samples from soil, fallen leaves, and atmospheric deposition at four forest sites east of Darmstadt, Germany, using advanced spectroscopic techniques. The findings indicate that tiny plastic particles settle on tree canopies—a process called the 'comb-out effect'—before being transferred to the forest floor via rainfall or autumn leaf fall in deciduous forests. Once on the ground, natural decomposition of leaf litter and biological activity, such as soil organisms, help incorporate these particles deeper into the soil layers, turning forests into long-term reservoirs for this pollution. The highest concentrations appear in the upper leaf litter, but notable amounts reach mineral soil horizons. Modeling suggests

item.title: Russia restricts access to Archive.today, a website for bypassing paywalls
item.description: Russian authorities have blocked the popular paywall bypass website Archive.today, along with its associated domains including .is and .ph. When accessed from Russia, visitors encounter a message stating that the resource is blocked by government decision, citing the federal censorship agency Roskomnadzor. While the official reason for the restriction has not been disclosed, Roskomnadzor confirmed that access to Archive.is is limited but provided no further explanation. Despite the block, the site remains accessible from other regions and users can still archive web pages. Archive.today allows users to save and view copies of websites, including content behind subscription or paywall restrictions. The website recently faced controversy when Wikipedia editors removed links to it, citing a discovery that Archive.today’s code directed visitors’ browsers to send automated traffic to a blogger critical of the service. There is currently no comment from Archive.today regarding the Russian bl

item.title: How Workplace Expectations of Health Deepen the Struggle for Employees Living with Chronic Pain
item.description: Nearly one in four adults in the United States lives with chronic pain, a growing issue that affects not only personal well-being but also the economy, with an estimated loss of $722 billion in productivity each year. A new study published in the Academy of Management Journal examined how professional expectations shape the daily experiences of workers living with long-term pain. Researchers interviewed 66 U.S. employees across various professions—including teachers, lawyers, police officers, and grocery store workers—who described feeling pressured to maintain an image of the 'ideal worker body'—healthy, active, and tireless. To avoid judgment and keep their jobs, many masked their pain, avoided visible coping measures, and pushed their bodies beyond safe limits. This led to worsening symptoms, diminished productivity, and in many cases, job loss or disability. However, some participants found healthier paths forward by seeking supportive employers and doctors who validated their cond

item.title: EY-Parthenon Estimates 40% U.S. Recession Risk with Potential for Quick Increase
item.description: In a Wall Street Journal live market coverage update dated March 23, 2026, EY-Parthenon, the strategy consulting arm of Ernst & Young, has raised its assessment of U.S. recession odds to 40% for the coming period. This forecast reflects growing economic pressures including persistent inflation, supply chain disruptions exacerbated by geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, and tightening financial conditions from recent Federal Reserve policy signals. Analysts at EY-Parthenon note that this probability could rapidly escalate if oil prices continue their upward trajectory or if trade policies under President Donald Trump lead to further market volatility. The report highlights how surging energy costs are squeezing consumer spending and corporate margins, potentially tipping the economy into contraction. Key indicators cited include slowing GDP growth projections below potential rates in the second half of 2026, weakening job market momentum, and rising bond yields signaling investor

item.title: Temporary Evacuation at Newark Airport After Burning Smell Detected
item.description: Operations at Newark Liberty International Airport in New Jersey were temporarily halted on Monday morning after air traffic controllers evacuated the control tower due to a reported burning smell. The incident began around 7:30 a.m. local time when personnel noticed smoke coming from an elevator. As a precaution, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) immediately ordered a ground stop, suspending both arrivals and departures. In a statement, the FAA explained that the evacuation was necessary to ensure the safety of controllers and flight operations. After an inspection determined that the issue was limited and posed no further hazard, controllers returned to the tower, and normal air traffic resumed. Newark Liberty International is one of the three major airports that serve the New York City area, ranking second in traffic volume behind John F. Kennedy International Airport. The disruptions caused by the evacuation were short-lived, and flight schedules soon stabilized. The mornin

item.title: UN Warns Earth Is Approaching Critical Climate Thresholds
item.description: The United Nations has declared that the Earth's climate is in a 'state of emergency,' citing unprecedented imbalances and escalating risks. The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) reports that from 2015 to 2025, the planet experienced its hottest 11 years on record, with 2025 ranking as the second or third hottest. Current measurements indicate that global temperatures are dangerously close to the 1.5°C warming threshold, recording 1.43°C last year. The report highlights the compounded effects of climate change, including extreme heatwaves, droughts, wildfires, floods, tropical cyclones, and other severe weather events that have caused widespread fatalities and economic damage. Rising greenhouse gas concentrations, including carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide, have reached levels not seen in at least 800,000 years. Heat accumulation in the oceans has intensified, driving long-term climate impacts for centuries. The Earth's energy imbalance has also reached record levels, f

item.title: SOM Designs Mountain-Inspired Towers for Kazakhstan’s New Planned City
item.description: The architectural firm Skidmore, Owings & Merrill (SOM) has unveiled plans for a striking new urban center in Kazakhstan, inspired by the surrounding Trans-Ili Alatau mountains. Known as Alatau, this city is currently being built from the ground up across 88,000 hectares near Almaty. At its heart will stand two architecturally bold towers that define a district envisioned as a future international hub for Central Asia. The taller of the two structures, measuring 272 meters, will feature a stepped, wedge-shaped design with glazed terraces and a central atrium that pulls daylight through the building’s core. The accompanying 80-meter hotel echoes this mountain-inspired form, both anchored by a three-story podium filled with retail, cultural, and public spaces. SOM’s design seeks to translate the topographical language of valleys, glaciers, and layered terrains into architectural form, linking the modern city directly with its natural environment. Though still in the conceptual phase, the

item.title: Missing Persons Crisis in Gaza Leaves Families in Legal and Financial Limbo
item.description:
The article examines the growing legal and humanitarian crisis in Gaza caused by the inability of thousands of families to obtain death certificates for missing relatives. Since October 2023, widespread bombardment, displacement, and detentions have severely disrupted civil registration systems that once made documenting deaths routine. As a result, many families are trapped in a legal gray zone, unable to prove whether their loved ones are dead or alive.
Research cited in the report estimates that more than 51,000 people may have gone missing at some point during the conflict, with up to 15,000 still unaccounted for. Without official confirmation of death, families face significant barriers: they cannot access bank accounts, pensions, inheritance, or humanitarian aid tied to the missing person. Women, in particular, are disproportionately affected, often left without financial support and vulnerable to exploitation.
Children of the missing are also at risk, frequently becoming what

item.title: Accelerated Coastal Erosion Prompts Concerns About Long-Term Protection for Sizewell C Nuclear Plant
item.description: Recent accelerated coastal erosion along the UK's east coast, exacerbated by a severe winter, has intensified debates about the safeguards for the £40 billion Sizewell C nuclear power station under construction on the Suffolk shoreline. The plant, adjacent to existing Sizewell A and B sites, is designed for over a century of operation and decommissioning, extending potentially to 2140 or beyond when spent fuel remains on-site. Critics highlight that the area ranks among Europe's fastest-eroding coastlines, with dozens of homes lost in nearby communities like Thorpeness, raising fears of unequal protection—millions invested in the plant's defenses while locals fend for themselves. Sizewell C asserts the site lies on a comparatively stable section between hard points, bolstered by the Sizewell-Dunwich Bank, with the facility elevated on a 7m platform and shielded by adaptable sea defenses exceeding 14m above current levels. These include temporary sheet piles during construction, transit

item.title: NIH Researchers Develop Improved Blood Test for Early Detection of Pancreatic Cancer
item.description: Researchers funded by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) have developed a blood test that could transform the early detection of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, one of the deadliest forms of cancer. Pancreatic cancer is often discovered only after it has spread, leaving few treatment options and resulting in survival rates below 10% after five years. The new test builds upon existing biomarkers and adds two newly identified proteins—aminopeptidase N (ANPEP) and polymeric immunoglobulin receptor (PIGR)—that show clear differences in people with early-stage pancreatic cancer. When combined with established markers CA19-9 and THBS2, the four-protein panel achieved a detection accuracy of 91.9%, correctly identifying early-stage cases about 87.5% of the time while maintaining a low false-positive rate of 5%. The results, published in *Clinical Cancer Research*, indicate that the test not only detects cancer with high sensitivity but also distinguishes it from non-cancerous condition

item.title: California Residents and Lawmakers Question Health and Energy Impacts of Proposed Data Centers
item.description: A proposed 950,000-square-foot data center in Imperial County, California, has sparked strong opposition from local residents due to concerns about air quality, rising utility costs, and potential health risks. The facility received an exemption from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), leaving residents without a formal environmental review and prompting debates among lawmakers about regulating AI-focused data centers. Residents like Fernanda Camarillo, who is asthmatic, fear that the center could worsen pollution and strain local power and water resources. Local community groups, including Not in My Backyard Imperial, are demanding transparency and proper CEQA assessments. California legislators, including Senator Steve Padilla, have introduced bills that would prevent future data centers from receiving CEQA exemptions, require companies to provide water and energy usage estimates, and impose tariffs for grid upgrades caused by these facilities. Experts warn that AI data

item.title: Media Control and the Illusion of Democratic Elections
item.description: This article, written in 2026, critiques the fairness of democratic elections, arguing that the system is inherently skewed by the control of media outlets by wealthy individuals and corporations. The author contends that this concentrated ownership creates an uneven playing field, effectively silencing alternative voices and manipulating public opinion. The piece draws parallels to a boxing match where one contestant is unfairly advantaged, highlighting the illusion of a truly balanced electoral process. The author asserts that the influence extends beyond overt control of candidates, resembling a 'sausage factory' model, especially prevalent in the United States. The core argument is that genuine democratic participation is impossible as long as the media – a vital 'fourth estate' – remains in the hands of a powerful economic elite. The author goes on to accuse the elite as external to the interests of common people, further underscoring the rigged nature of the elections and the res

item.title: Privacy Concerns and Public Harassment Linked to Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses
item.description: The Ray-Ban Meta smart glasses, developed by Meta, are becoming increasingly popular but also controversial due to privacy concerns. Users can record video from their point of view, which has led to incidents of public harassment, particularly targeting women. Influencers on social media, such as Sayed Kaghazi and Cameron John, use the glasses to film attempts to flirt with strangers in public spaces, leading to the nickname “pervert glasses.” In addition to these behavioral concerns, the glasses automatically upload footage to Meta, where contract workers may review sensitive content, prompting legal scrutiny. Lawmakers in the U.S., including Senators Ron Wyden, Ed Markey, and Jeff Merkley, have questioned Meta about integrating face-recognition technology, highlighting risks of stalking, harassment, and privacy violations. Users are also finding ways to bypass the glasses’ recording indicator, creating a black market for stealth modifications. On the defensive side, some developers h

item.title: Four Jewish Volunteer Ambulances Destroyed in Suspected Antisemitic Arson in London
item.description: Several ambulances belonging to the Jewish volunteer rescue organization Hatzola Northwest were deliberately set on fire outside the Machzikei Hadath synagogue in Golders Green, north London, early Monday morning. The attack, which occurred around 1:36 a.m., caused multiple explosions as gas canisters aboard the vehicles ignited, lighting up the night sky and alarming local residents. Police confirmed they are investigating the incident as an antisemitic hate crime, led by counter-terrorism officers, though it has not officially been classified as a terrorist act. Security footage reportedly shows three masked individuals setting one of the ambulances on fire before fleeing. Four of Hatzola’s six ambulances were destroyed, each valued at about £125,000. Hatzola Northwest chairman Shloimie Richman described the arson as a targeted attack on the local Jewish community, while volunteers expressed shock that a life-saving organization could be the focus of such hostility. British Prime Min

item.title: Cursor's Composer 2 Model Uses Moonshot AI’s Kimi as a Base, Enhanced with Additional Training
item.description: Cursor, a U.S.-based AI coding company, recently launched Composer 2, a model touted as offering advanced coding capabilities. Shortly after the release, an X user highlighted that Composer 2 was largely based on Moonshot AI’s open-source Kimi 2.5, a Chinese AI model supported by Alibaba and HongShan. Cursor initially did not mention this connection, which raised questions about transparency. Cursor's VP of developer education, Lee Robinson, confirmed that Composer 2 started from the open-source Kimi base, but clarified that only about a quarter of the computing for the final model came from Kimi, with the majority resulting from Cursor's own training. He also emphasized that Composer 2’s performance differs significantly from Kimi’s due to these enhancements. Both Cursor and Moonshot AI confirmed that the use of Kimi complied with licensing terms, with the latter praising Cursor for integrating their model within a commercial partnership. The decision not to mention Kimi upfront may h

item.title: A Gaza Teen’s Disappearance and the Mass Crisis of the Missing in Gaza
item.description: The article follows the story of 16‑year‑old Hassan, an autistic teenager from Gaza, who vanished in 2024 after riding his bicycle from his family’s home during the war. His parents, Abeer and Ali, have spent months and years searching hospitals, morgues, shelters, and informal networks, only to find no trace of him. Hassan is not confirmed dead, detained, or alive; he exists in a state of unresolved uncertainty that mirrors the broader crisis of thousands of missing Palestinians in Gaza. The text describes how Israel’s blockade has long prevented Gaza from acquiring basic forensic tools—such as DNA testing and biometric databases—so bodies cannot be reliably identified, and many people simply disappear into rubble, detention, or mass graves with no official records. Hamas‑run health authorities, the Palestinian Center for the Missing, and the International Committee of the Red Cross estimate that between roughly 9,000 and 15,000 people may still be missing, while a rudimentary forensi

item.title: Jony Ive Designs a Minimalist Light-Based Clock with Balmuda
item.description: After three decades defining the look and feel of modern technology, renowned designer Jony Ive has turned his focus to an object rooted in tradition—the clock. In partnership with Tokyo-based design company Balmuda, Ive’s studio has created 'The Clock,' a compact 75mm square piece machined from a single block of aluminum and weighing roughly seven ounces. While it draws inspiration from the elegant simplicity of pocket watches, the innovation lies in its complete elimination of hands. Instead, The Clock conveys time through light, using a 'Light Hour' dial that gently sweeps across its face in motion reminiscent of the Foucault pendulum displayed at Tokyo’s National Museum of Nature and Science. This design invites users to sense the passage of time in a calm, atmospheric way rather than reading it precisely through mechanical means, resembling how a sundial reflects natural rhythms. The device offers three operational modes—Relax Time, which diffuses soothing ambient soundscapes; Foc

item.title: Rising costs and delays doom UK’s new nuclear power plans
item.description:
The article examines the UK’s proposed new nuclear power program and argues that it is economically and practically failing. The Hinkley Point C nuclear project in Somerset, which uses two French Evolutionary Power Reactor (EPR) units of 1,630 MW each, has seen its original estimated cost nearly triple from about 24 billion dollars to almost 67 billion dollars, with completion pushed back to at least 2030—13 years beyond the original schedule. The original agreed strike price of 123.50 dollars per megawatt‑hour is also set to rise each year with inflation, contradicting government claims that new nuclear will lower household bills.
Shortly after these cost hikes were announced for Hinkley, projections for the Sizewell C project—a twin EPR plant planned for the Suffolk coast—showed that its electricity could cost almost double today’s prices, around 160 dollars per megawatt‑hour, according to the UK government’s own report. Remarkably, that same report assumes no further cost escalatio

item.title: Long-term Caspase-2 inhibition in liver may increase cancer risk despite short-term benefits
item.description: A recent study from the University of Adelaide reveals potential long-term risks associated with blocking the enzyme Caspase-2, a strategy previously thought to help treat fatty liver disease. While Caspase-2 inhibitors can reduce fat accumulation in the liver and may offer short-term benefits, researchers found that the absence of this enzyme in genetically modified mice caused abnormal liver cell growth, increased genetic damage, and heightened inflammation. Over time, these changes led to liver scarring and significantly higher rates of liver cancer, particularly in older mice. The findings suggest that Caspase-2 plays a critical role in maintaining liver cell stability and removing damaged cells, challenging the assumption that inhibiting it is universally beneficial. The study emphasizes the need for caution in developing therapies targeting Caspase-2, as short-term improvements in fatty liver disease may come with dangerous long-term consequences, including increased susceptibili

item.title: Habermas and the crisis of democratic deliberation in the digital age
item.description: The article reflects on the relevance of Jürgen Habermas’s theory of deliberative democracy in contemporary societies dominated by digital media, polarization, and algorithmic attention economies. It begins by noting that many now see Habermas’s ideal of rational public deliberation as outdated or even naive, given the fragmentation of public discourse into ideological tribes whose communication is driven more by emotional appeal and manipulation than by reasoned argument. The media landscape of social networks, targeted propaganda, and competition for clicks is portrayed as fundamentally at odds with the social conditions Habermas assumed for a functioning public sphere. The piece also acknowledges that politics has always been a struggle for power, identity, and belonging, and that Habermas’s normative model, while appealing, does not describe how most societies actually operate today. Yet the author argues that the Habermasian ideal remains crucial as a moral and normative horizon:

item.title: GrapheneOS Maintains Privacy-Focused OS Without Age Verification Requirements
item.description: GrapheneOS, an Android-based operating system designed with privacy in mind, has publicly stated that it will not implement age verification measures as required by new laws, such as Brazil's Digital ECA (Law 15.211), which imposes significant fines on noncompliant OS providers. The project emphasizes that its operating system will remain usable worldwide without requiring personal information, identification, or user accounts. This decision could limit sales in regions with strict regulations, but GrapheneOS suggests that users could still install the OS independently on devices purchased in those regions. Recently, GrapheneOS announced a partnership with Motorola to expand the OS to future Motorola devices, breaking its previous exclusivity with Google Pixel phones. However, compliance with local laws would fall on Motorola for preinstalled devices. GrapheneOS joins other open-source projects, such as DB48X and MidnightBSD, in rejecting mandatory age verification requirements. The st

item.title: Nvidia Showcases Olaf Robot Demo at GTC Highlighting Robotics Challenges
item.description: At Nvidia's 2026 GTC conference, CEO Jensen Huang presented a wide-ranging keynote covering advanced graphics technologies, AI strategies, and robotics. A notable highlight was a robot version of Disney's Olaf from 'Frozen,' demonstrating Nvidia's robotics capabilities. During the Equity podcast discussion, TechCrunch editors debated both the technological innovations and social considerations of such projects. While the Olaf robot impressed with its engineering, its microphone had to be cut when it began to ramble, raising questions about real-world interactions, such as potential mishaps in public spaces like Disney parks. The discussion emphasized that while these demos showcase technical prowess, they often overlook social challenges, safety, and user experience. Nvidia's promotion of open-source projects like NemoClaw reflects a strategic move to maintain relevance in enterprise AI and robotics, signaling that innovation combined with practical deployment strategies is critical. A

item.title: Israel tightens outgoing flight restrictions amid heightened missile threat from Iran
item.description: Israel has significantly tightened restrictions on outgoing commercial flights as the country faces an elevated missile threat in the context of ongoing hostilities with Iran. The Israeli Civil Aviation Authority has informed airlines that security conditions in the region may deteriorate rapidly, prompting new limits on departures from major Israeli airports. Authorities warned that airspace could be closed on short notice, echoing prior precedent during past confrontations with Iran, which had forced the grounding of large numbers of civilian flights. The move reflects broader anxiety about the fragility of regional air routes, as missile exchanges and drone attacks have already disrupted major hubs across the Middle East and forced airlines to reroute or cancel numerous services. International carriers are being advised to prepare for further schedule changes and potential delays, underscoring how the deeper risk of expanded conflict continues to ripple through global aviation netwo

item.title: Live Updates: Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq Performance on March 23, 2026
item.description: The Wall Street Journal's live coverage tracks real-time movements in major U.S. stock indices on March 23, 2026, focusing on the Dow Jones Industrial Average, S&P 500, and Nasdaq Composite. As markets opened amid ongoing economic uncertainties, including recent Federal Reserve signals on interest rates and global trade tensions under President Trump's administration, the Dow hovered near record highs but faced volatility from tech sector pressures. The S&P 500, representing broader market health, showed modest gains driven by strong earnings from financial and energy sectors, while the Nasdaq dipped slightly due to profit-taking in high-growth tech stocks like those in AI and semiconductors. Key highlights include a surge in bank stocks following positive economic data releases, contrasted by declines in consumer discretionary names amid inflation concerns. Throughout the day, analysts noted investor caution ahead of upcoming corporate earnings season and potential policy shifts. Bond

item.title: Brainstem Region Linked to High Blood Pressure: New Insights and Treatment Possibilities
item.description: Researchers from the University of Auckland have identified a surprising brain-based cause of high blood pressure, focusing on a small area in the brainstem known as the lateral parafacial region. This region, traditionally responsible for controlling breathing, also activates nerves that constrict blood vessels and raise blood pressure. In their study, the researchers demonstrated that inactivation of this brain region led to a normalization of blood pressure in experimental subjects, indicating its potential role in hypertension. The study highlights the connection between forceful breathing (like during exercise or coughing) and high blood pressure, suggesting that certain breathing patterns may be a key factor in some cases of hypertension. Researchers also explored a potential treatment targeting the carotid bodies, which send signals to the brainstem, offering a safer alternative to directly affecting the brain. This discovery could lead to new treatments for hypertension, especi

item.title: Iran formally urges Jordan not to allow its territory or airspace to be used for military strikes
item.description: Iran has formally requested that Jordan refrain from allowing its territory or airspace to be used for any military attacks, amid rising regional tensions involving the United States, Israel, and Iran. The appeal comes as part of a broader Iranian diplomatic effort to prevent regional actors from facilitating cross‑border operations that could further escalate the ongoing wider conflict. Jordanian officials have reaffirmed Amman’s policy of non‑involvement, emphasizing that the kingdom will not permit its land or skies to serve as a launching point for strikes against Iran or other regional states. Foreign Minister Ayman Safadi reportedly conveyed this stance in a phone call with his Iranian counterpart, underscoring Jordan’s commitment to protecting its sovereignty and regional stability. The exchange underlines Jordan’s attempt to balance its security cooperation with Western partners while avoiding direct entanglement in the expanding Iran–U.S.–Israel confrontation. The development

item.title: Federal Judge Strikes Down Pentagon's Press Credential Restrictions as Unconstitutional
item.description: A federal judge in Washington, DC, ruled in favor of the New York Times, overturning the Pentagon's restrictive policies on press credentials that had evicted major outlets like the New York Times, Washington Post, Wall Street Journal, ABC, NBC, CBS, and Fox News. Judge Paul Friedman declared the rules unconstitutional, violating the First Amendment by suppressing political speech and targeting disfavored journalists while favoring pro-administration figures such as Mike Lindell, Matt Gaetz, Laura Loomer, and James O'Keefe. The policy, implemented under War Secretary Pete Hegseth last October, allowed the Department of War to classify reporters as security risks and required them to source information only from authorized officials, limiting coverage to favorable narratives. This ruling comes amid the U.S. war with Iran, where journalists have pressed unanswered questions about the conflict, including Iran's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, protection of Americans from drones, and ris

item.title: Congressional Black Caucus Criticizes Pritzker Over Illinois Senate Primary Support
item.description: Illinois Governor JB Pritzker faces backlash from Congressional Black Caucus (CBC) members following the Democratic primary for the state's open Senate seat. Pritzker provided significant financial support, including $5 million from his super PAC, to Lieutenant Governor Juliana Stratton, helping her secure victory over Rep. Robin Kelly, the CBC's preferred candidate who finished third. CBC leaders like Rep. Bennie Thompson and Rep. Joyce Beatty expressed frustration, stating Pritzker must justify his actions, especially as he considers a 2028 presidential run where Black voter support is crucial. They emphasized that the path to the Democratic nomination runs through the CBC, and some indicated reluctance to mend fences first. Rep. Gregory Meeks, who chairs the CBC PAC, said others would need to reach out to them amid midterm priorities. Pritzker's team defended the support, highlighting Stratton's historic potential as one of few Black women senators and their long partnership. While

item.title: Trump's Counterterrorism Director Resigns, Citing Unfounded Justification for War on Iran
item.description: Joe Kent, Director of the National Counterterrorism Center, resigned in protest over the U.S. military action against Iran, asserting that Iran posed no immediate threat to the United States. Kent criticized President Donald Trump for allegedly being influenced by Israeli officials and American pro-Israel lobbying groups to initiate the conflict. Despite repeated intelligence reports, including testimony by Tulsi Gabbard, confirming that Iran was not actively developing nuclear weapons, Trump proceeded with attacks, which Kent claims were unjustified and harmful. Kent, a former Army Ranger and CIA paramilitary officer, emphasized that sending American troops into a war serving no strategic benefit was unacceptable, particularly after the loss of his first wife in a bombing in Syria. While Kent has been a staunch Trump loyalist, his resignation is significant as it highlights internal dissent over Trump’s foreign policy. Critics have noted Kent's controversial past and associations, but

item.title: Microsoft Insiders Push to Remove Microsoft Account Requirement for Windows 11
item.description: Microsoft has received criticism for its mandatory Microsoft account requirement for installing Windows 11. While the company has made efforts to address other complaints, the account stipulation remains. However, reports indicate that some Microsoft insiders, including Vice President Scott Hanselman, are pushing to relax this requirement. Hanselman himself expressed frustration over the policy, stating on social media that he is working on it. Despite technical feasibility, the change has not yet been officially confirmed, and the discussion remains ongoing within the company. There’s also a larger sentiment in the tech community calling for less aggressive upselling, such as promoting Microsoft Edge and Bing, and reducing unwanted ads in Windows. The outcome is uncertain, but Microsoft faces growing pressure to listen to user feedback on this matter, with a potential shift expected sometime this year.

item.title: Walmart to Implement Digital Price Tags Across All U.S. Stores by 2026
item.description: Walmart is set to replace its paper price tags with digital shelf labels (DSLs) across all U.S. stores by the end of 2026. These new digital tags promise to increase operational efficiency by reducing the time spent on manual price updates, with employees like Amanda Bailey in Ohio reporting a 75% reduction in time spent on pricing duties. The digital labels will also assist Walmart's Spark delivery drivers in locating items more easily. According to experts, these labels can help reduce pricing discrepancies and align in-store and online promotions more accurately, ensuring consumers pay the price they see. However, some concerns have been raised regarding dynamic pricing, and some U.S. states are considering banning such practices. Despite the fears of potential price fluctuations, experts argue that digital labels can reduce food waste by marking down perishable items in real-time. Legislation aimed at curbing dynamic pricing is already being proposed in several states, with some cr

item.title: Sound-Levitated Time Crystal Demonstrates Nonreciprocal Particle Interactions
item.description: Researchers at New York University have developed a novel type of time crystal using sound waves to levitate tiny styrofoam beads. Unlike traditional systems that adhere to Newton's Third Law of Motion, this setup exhibits nonreciprocal interactions where particles affect each other unevenly. The beads are suspended in mid-air using an acoustic levitator, and their oscillations create a steady repeating rhythm, observable to the naked eye within a compact device about one foot tall. This simple yet elegant system not only provides a new platform for exploring fundamental physics but also offers potential applications in quantum computing, advanced data storage, and understanding biological timing mechanisms like circadian rhythms. The larger beads scatter more sound than smaller ones, creating asymmetrical forces that enable self-sustained oscillations, demonstrating an unusual form of symmetry breaking. This research could pave the way for more practical uses of time crystals in techn

item.title: Foreign National Indicted for Alleged $90 Million Medicare Fraud in the U.S.
item.description: Anar Rustamov, a 38-year-old foreign national from Azerbaijan, has been indicted by a federal grand jury for allegedly participating in a $90 million Medicare Advantage fraud scheme. According to the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Northern District of California, Rustamov used a company he created, Dublin Helping Hand, to submit thousands of fraudulent claims for medical equipment, including blood glucose monitors and orthotic braces, between October 2024 and June 2025. The claims were made on behalf of unsuspecting patients, who did not require the equipment and did not authorize the submissions. Additionally, the medical providers listed on the claims did not approve them. Rustamov, who previously lived in Sunnyvale, California, may have entered the United States illegally and remains at large. Federal authorities emphasize that the alleged scheme diverted taxpayer funds intended to assist patients genuinely in need. If convicted, Rustamov could face up to 20 years in prison for each

item.title: No. 1 seed Florida falls to Iowa as March Madness second round delivers buzzer‑beaters and upsets
item.description: Sunday’s slate of March Madness second‑round games delivered a wave of drama as the first No. 1 seed of the 2026 tournament fell and multiple teams booked their trips to the Sweet 16. In the men’s bracket, No. 9 Iowa knocked off defending champion No. 1 Florida in a tense 73‑72 thriller, with Alvaro Folgueiras hitting a game‑winning shot with 8.9 seconds left. Iowa will now face Nebraska in the Sweet 16, while Florida’s bid for back‑to‑back titles ended earlier than expected. St. John’s also advanced after guard Dylan Darling converted a buzzer‑beater layup to defeat No. 4 Kansas 67‑65, marking the Red Storm’s first Sweet 16 appearance since 1999. No. 6 Tennessee moved on to its fourth straight Sweet 16 by holding off No. 3 Virginia’s late comeback that came down to missed shots and a costly turnover in the final seconds. Purdue survived a scare against Miami, rallying after trailing at halftime to win 79‑69 and extend to its third straight Sweet 16 as coach Matt Painter recorded his 5

item.title: NASA’s Webb Telescope Discovers Unexpected Atmosphere on Super Earth TOI-561 b
item.description: Astronomers using NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) have uncovered a surprising discovery about the planet TOI-561 b, an ancient and ultra-hot super Earth. Previously thought to be a barren 'lava world', the planet is surrounded by an unexpectedly thick atmosphere. Despite its extreme proximity to its star and a surface likely covered in molten rock, JWST's observations suggest that the planet is far cooler than anticipated, hinting at a heat-distributing atmosphere. TOI-561 b completes a full orbit around its star in just over 10 hours and has one side locked in perpetual daylight. This discovery challenges prior assumptions about planets in ultra-short orbits, which were expected to lose their atmosphere over time. Additionally, the planet's relatively low density further supports the idea of a thick, volatile-rich atmosphere. JWST's temperature data reveals that the planet's dayside is cooler than expected, a clue that heat is being transferred from the dayside to the nightsi

item.title: Trump and UK’s Starmer agree on need to reopen Strait of Hormuz amid Middle East conflict
item.description: U.S. President Donald Trump and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer held a phone call to discuss the ongoing crisis in the Middle East, with a focus on efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a key maritime chokepoint for global oil shipments. According to a Downing Street spokeswoman, the two leaders emphasized the strategic importance of restoring safe passage through the strait, which has been largely blocked amid the broader Iran–U.S.–Israel conflict. Starmer reportedly offered condolences for American military personnel killed in the fighting, underscoring the human toll of the hostilities. The leaders agreed to maintain regular communication as the situation evolves, reflecting a broader effort by Western powers to coordinate policy toward Iran and regional security. Trump has repeatedly urged allied countries, including China, Japan, South Korea, and European nations, to contribute naval assets to help secure the strait, arguing that major oil importers should share responsibili

item.title: PowerBall Jackpot Won on March 20, 2026, Resets to R57 Million for March 24 Draw
item.description: The South African lottery saw a major development with the PowerBall jackpot of over R108 million won in the Friday, March 20, 2026, draw, after 15 consecutive rollovers without a winner. This victory ended a long buildup that had pushed the prize to significant heights, marking a positive outcome for the lucky ticket holder. However, it means the jackpot resets for the following Tuesday, March 24, 2026, draw. Ithuba, the national lottery operator, guarantees R52 million for the main PowerBall game and R5 million for PowerBall Plus, for a combined total of R57 million in potential winnings. The article recaps the March 20 winning numbers: main PowerBall numbers 05, 08, 11, 17, 41 with PowerBall 02, and PowerBall Plus numbers 12, 18, 28, 40, 45 with PowerBall 15. It notes that 2026 has already featured a high payout of R179 million in January, the second-largest in history after a R232 million win in 2019. Players are reminded that each draw is independent, with odds of 1 in 42,310,200

item.title: SpaceX’s AI Sat Mini: orbital data centers with 180‑meter solar arrays
item.description: SpaceX is developing a new series of satellites called AI Sat Mini that will function as orbital data centers powered by large solar arrays roughly 180 meters long, each designed to generate around 100 kilowatts of electrical power. These satellites are part of Elon Musk’s broader vision to move a major share of AI computing capacity into space, leveraging low‑Earth orbit and Starship’s heavy‑lift capabilities to launch tens of millions of tons of computing hardware annually. The chips for these satellites will be the custom D3 processors built by Terafab, a semiconductor project Musk recently unveiled with up to 25 billion dollars in planned investment. Musk argues that space‑based data centers can exploit the Sun’s nearly limitless energy and avoid the cost of acquiring terrestrial land, positioning them in sun‑synchronous polar orbits to maximize illumination. However, the project faces serious technical hurdles, including thermal management in the vacuum of space and the need for u

item.title: SEC Closes Four-Year Investigation into Faraday Future Without Enforcement Action
item.description: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission has closed its nearly four-year investigation into electric vehicle startup Faraday Future, despite staff recommendations last year to pursue enforcement action. Multiple sources confirmed the agency notified the company and involved parties this past week that no action would be taken against Faraday Future or its executives, including founder Jia Yueting. The probe examined potential false and misleading statements during the company's 2021 SPAC merger and allegations of faked sales of its first FF91 vehicles in 2023, raised by whistleblowers. Investigators issued subpoenas, conducted depositions of former employees and executives in 2024 and 2025, and sent Wells Notices in July 2025 signaling a preliminary intent to recommend charges related to anti-fraud provisions, particularly around related-party transactions and Jia's control over the company. Faraday Future cooperated extensively, spending significant resources, and expressed relief

item.title: Tom Brady Praises Logan Paul's Performance in Flag Football Classic
item.description: NFL legend Tom Brady offered high praise for WWE star Logan Paul after the Fanatics Flag Football Classic on March 21, 2026. Despite a heated feud between the two leading up to the game, Brady expressed respect for Paul's dedication and drive on the field. Brady noted that Paul impressed him with his seriousness and commitment to improving, even citing how Paul actively sought to learn from coaches. Brady also reflected on Paul's and his brother Jake's relentless drive to succeed in their respective fields, acknowledging their determination to make things happen rather than wait for opportunities. Although Brady's team, the Founders FFC, was defeated by Team USA 43-16, he showed he still had the skills to compete at a high level. The game had been a part of a broader push to promote flag football's inclusion in the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics, with some NFL players eyeing spots on the national team. Despite the loss, Brady remained positive about the experience and Paul's efforts.

item.title: Ukraine Accused of Striking Russian Civilian Targets Including Hospitals and Kindergartens Amid Iran Conflict
item.description: According to a report originally published by RT and reposted on nuclear-news.net, Ukrainian forces have carried out multiple strikes on Russian territory and Russian-controlled areas in March 2026, resulting in significant civilian casualties. The article claims that at least 23 Russian civilians were killed and many more injured in attacks since late February, coinciding with the escalation of the US-Israeli conflict with Iran, which it suggests may be diverting international attention. Key incidents include a March 10 Ukrainian drone strike on a hospital in the Donetsk People’s Republic (DPR) that killed ten medical personnel and injured others, as well as a Storm Shadow missile attack (supplied by the UK) on the city of Bryansk, killing at least seven civilians and wounding 42, with Russian sources alleging direct British military involvement due to the weapon's complexity. Additional reports detail a March 8 attack in the DPR that killed a family of four—including a six-year-old b

item.title: Ventura County Teen Survives Rattlesnake Bite on Wendy Trail
item.description: A teenage girl from Ventura County was rescued after being bitten by a rattlesnake on the Wendy Trail in Newbury Park on Friday evening. Firefighters responded to the call at 7:30 pm and trekked up the trail to reach the girl. They used a special wheeled tool to bring her down safely and transferred her to an ambulance for hospital transport. At the time, she was reported in stable condition with minor injuries. This incident is part of a concerning trend of increasing rattlesnake bites in the region. Just a week earlier, Ventura County Fire officials responded to a similar incident at Wildwood Regional Park near Thousand Oaks. While deaths from rattlesnake bites are rare, this rise in encounters is raising awareness about safety on local hiking trails. Experts suggest wearing proper hiking boots, staying on marked paths, and avoiding tall grass to reduce the risk of snake encounters. As temperatures warm up, wildlife activity increases, including rattlesnakes, making caution in outdoo

item.title: US Counterterrorism Director Resigns Over Iran War, Citing Israeli Influence
item.description:
In a significant development amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict, Joe Kent, director of the National Counterterrorism Center, resigned on March 18, 2026, becoming the first senior Trump administration official to step down over the war. In his resignation letter, Kent stated he could not support the conflict in good conscience, asserting that Iran posed no imminent threat to the United States and that the war was initiated due to pressure from Israel and its American lobby. Kent, a veteran with a controversial background including past associations with far-right groups, highlighted concerns that the conflict serves Israeli interests more than American ones.
The article, drawing from a piece by Robert Kuttner in The American Prospect, details how Israeli actions have escalated tensions without full prior consultation with President Trump. Israeli strikes assassinated Iranian security chief Ali Larijani and intelligence minister Esmail Khatib, with Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz pu

item.title: 67,800-Year-Old Hand Stencil in Sulawesi Cave Dated as World's Oldest Known Rock Art
item.description: Researchers from Griffith University, Indonesia's BRIN, and Southern Cross University have identified a hand stencil in Liang Metanduno cave on Muna Island, southeastern Sulawesi, Indonesia, as the oldest reliably dated rock art in the world, with a minimum age of 67,800 years. The artwork, a partial hand stencil modified to have a claw-like appearance by narrowing the finger outlines, was dated using uranium-series analysis of overlying calcite layers. This discovery surpasses previous Sulawesi rock art dates by at least 15,000 years and exceeds the prior global record (a Neanderthal-attributed stencil from Spain) by over 1,000 years. The site shows continuous artistic activity spanning at least 35,000 years, until around 20,000 years ago, highlighting Sulawesi as home to one of the earliest and longest-lasting artistic traditions. The findings support the long chronology for human arrival in Sahul (the ancient landmass including Australia, New Guinea, and Tasmania), suggesting ancest

item.title: A Monthly Newspaper Dedicated Exclusively to Positive News
item.description: In today’s fast-paced media environment, most news apps bombard readers with negative headlines, creating stress and anxiety. To counter this, the publication Good Good Good launched the Goodnewspaper, a physical monthly newspaper entirely focused on positive news. Each issue features uplifting stories about scientific breakthroughs, acts of kindness, environmental progress, and animals being rescued—topics that rarely reach mainstream headlines because they do not provoke urgency or fear. Beyond celebrating good news, the newspaper provides actionable steps for readers to get involved, transforming passive hope into concrete actions. Themed editions include Sustainability, Mental Health, Veterans, Refugees, and Animals, emphasizing both specific issues and general human decency. Printed on recycled paper with soy-based inks, Good Good Good also donates 1% of all sales to environmental nonprofits, highlighting their commitment to ethical journalism. The tactile experience of flipping t

item.title: Chappell Roan responds to incident involving security guard and footballer Jorginho’s daughter
item.description: American pop singer Chappell Roan expressed sadness after reports that her security guard allegedly spoke aggressively to the wife and daughter of footballer Jorginho in a hotel in São Paulo, Brazil. According to Jorginho, the 34-year-old former Chelsea and Arsenal midfielder who now plays for Flamengo, his 11-year-old daughter was left shaken and cried following the incident. The athlete posted on Instagram criticizing the singer, claiming the guard’s behavior upset his family and remarking that she should remember her dependence on fans. In response, Roan released a video to her eight million social media followers explaining that the guard in question was not part of her personal team and that she was unaware of any interaction with the family. She clarified that she had been quietly having breakfast at her hotel and did not see anyone approach her. Roan apologized to the mother and child, emphasizing her regret that they felt uncomfortable, and insisted she does not harbor negative

item.title: Skateboarder Andy Anderson Showcases Paris in New Short Film
item.description: A new short film, 'The Shape of Paris,' offers a unique and artistic perspective on the city, celebrating both its iconic landmarks and the thriving skateboarding scene. Canadian skateboarder Andy Anderson takes center stage, showcasing his unconventional style that blends old-school freestyle tricks with modern street skating. Filmed by Brett Novak, the film moves beyond typical trick compilations, presenting a visually stunning piece that feels like a short art film. Novak’s camera work, combined with pristine color grading, transforms Paris into an impossibly crisp and vibrant backdrop for Anderson’s performance. The film highlights the city's familiar architecture while also revealing hidden pockets and unexpected spaces perfect for skateboarding. A charming detail in the credits lists the 'trick acknowledgements,' a novel approach mirroring how movies credit stunts – a testament to the film's creative spirit. The film's quality has garnered significant praise, as evidenced by comm

item.title: Óscar Vara critica las políticas que dificultan el acceso a la vivienda en España
item.description: Óscar Vara, profesor de Economía de la Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, alertó sobre la difícil situación del mercado de la vivienda en España. Durante su intervención en laSexta Xplica, Vara destacó que las políticas de control de precios y la falta de desarrollo urbanístico han provocado que cada vez sea más complicado acceder a una vivienda. Según el economista, estas medidas han expulsado a quienes desean alquilar y han generado restricciones que impiden ofrecer soluciones efectivas. Vara manifestó su asombro ante la insistencia de continuar con políticas que, a su juicio, empeoran la situación, subrayando que la escasez de terreno disponible y la falta de reformas urbanísticas agravan el problema. El profesor advirtió de forma contundente que, si se mantiene este enfoque, muchas personas tendrán enormes dificultades para conseguir una vivienda propia en España. La intervención de Vara busca concienciar sobre la necesidad de replantear las estrategias públicas en el sector inmobilia

item.title: Aumento de agresiones a personas LGTBI en España durante el último año
item.description: Los delitos de odio contra personas LGTBI en España han registrado un aumento significativo en los últimos doce meses. Según los datos recopilados, un 16,3% de este colectivo ha sido víctima de agresiones físicas o verbales simplemente por su orientación sexual o identidad de género. Los incidentes se producen en diversas ciudades del país, desde Madrid hasta León y Alicante, y van desde palizas hasta ataques con objetos, reflejando un clima de creciente hostilidad. Ronny de la Cruz, presidente de COGAM, advierte que estos hechos no son aislados y están impulsados por discursos públicos que fomentan la homofobia. La violencia genera miedo e inseguridad entre quienes solo desean vivir con libertad y seguridad, mostrando que todavía persisten barreras sociales importantes para la diversidad sexual en España. Expertos y colectivos piden medidas más efectivas de prevención y protección, así como un cambio en la narrativa política y mediática que permita reducir este tipo de agresiones y ga

item.title: Líderes de la extrema derecha europea toman distancia de Trump por el conflicto en Irán
item.description: Desde el inicio de la guerra en Irán, varios líderes de la extrema derecha europea, tradicionalmente aliados de Donald Trump, han comenzado a distanciarse de sus políticas. La primera ministra italiana, Giorgia Meloni, ha declarado públicamente que Italia no forma parte del conflicto y ha criticado que la intervención de Estados Unidos e Israel vulnera el derecho internacional. Marine Le Pen, líder de la Agrupación Nacional en Francia, ha sido especialmente crítica con las acciones de Trump, calificándolas de erráticas y cuestionando los objetivos de la guerra. En Alemania, el partido Alternativa para Alemania (AfD) muestra divisiones internas respecto al apoyo al trumpismo y la implicación de la OTAN. En el Reino Unido, Nigel Farage, quien había sido uno de los principales defensores de Trump, se muestra contrario a involucrarse en un conflicto extranjero. Viktor Orban, primer ministro húngaro, también ha abogado por la vía diplomática y ha alertado sobre posibles consecuencias global

item.title: Óscar Puente responde a Aguirre destacando la corrupción durante su etapa en Madrid
item.description: Las recientes declaraciones de Esperanza Aguirre en 'elPeriódico' culpando a Mariano Rajoy del auge de Vox han generado una rápida respuesta política. Aguirre afirmó que Vox existe porque Rajoy incumplió el programa del Partido Popular, recordando cómo heredó un partido unificado de Aznar y lo dejó dividido para Pablo Casado. La reacción más directa vino del ministro de Transportes, Óscar Puente, quien mediante un mensaje en redes sociales replicó con ironía que en el tema de la corrupción, Rajoy lo había hecho tan bien como Aguirre. La respuesta de Puente ha cambiado el foco del debate desde la estrategia política hacia los casos de corrupción que salpicaron a colaboradores cercanos de Aguirre durante su presidencia en la Comunidad de Madrid (2003-2012). Entre los casos más destacados están el de Alfredo Prada en la Ciudad de la Justicia, el caso Gürtel con Alberto López Viejo, la macrocausa Púnica que afectó a Francisco Granados y el caso Lezo vinculado a Ignacio González. Aunque Agu

item.title: Brecha salarial en España: Los trabajadores generan riqueza pero reciben una fracción
item.description: Un informe reciente de Analistas Económicos de Andalucía (AEA) revela una alarmante brecha entre la productividad de los trabajadores en España y sus respectivos salarios. Según los datos, cada trabajador genera una media de 262.730 euros al año, mientras que su salario anual se sitúa en apenas 39.900 euros. Esta disparidad es especialmente evidente en comparación con otras economías europeas y plantea serias preocupaciones sobre la distribución de la riqueza y la sostenibilidad del modelo económico español. El estudio destaca cómo las empresas se benefician de la alta productividad de la fuerza laboral, mientras que los trabajadores reciben una proporción significativamente menor de la riqueza generada. La situación varía según la industria y la región, pero en general, el patrón de desproporción persiste. La investigación de AEA invita a un debate sobre la necesidad de políticas que garanticen una compensación justa para los trabajadores y promuevan una distribución más equitativa de

item.title: Descubren en un mosaico romano del siglo III la representación única de una mujer cazadora de animales en la arena
item.description: Un mosaico romano, descubierto en 1860 en Reims, Francia, y destruido en la Primera Guerra Mundial, ha sido reexaminado recientemente por el investigador Alfonso Mañas de la Universidad de California, Berkeley. Este mosaico de grandes dimensiones mostraba escenas del anfiteatro con gladiadores, animales salvajes y cacerías escenificadas. Una figura que antes se creía masculina o ambigua ha sido identificada como una mujer venatrix, cazadora entrenada para participar en venationes, es decir, cacerías de animales en la arena. A diferencia de las mujeres condenadas a morir ante bestias, esta figura sostiene un látigo y participa activamente en la caza, indicando que era una profesional y no una víctima. La datación del mosaico en el siglo III CE amplía el periodo conocido de participación femenina en espectáculos romanos más allá de los siglos I y II, desafiando ideas previas sobre su desaparición temprana. Este hallazgo también sugiere que la caza de animales era socialmente más aceptabl

item.title: La pediatría en Cabueñes: retos, logros y la influencia de la inmigración en la natalidad de Gijón
item.description: Nuria Fernández González, jefa del servicio de Pediatría del Hospital Universitario de Cabueñes desde 2018, destaca la evolución y los logros del servicio durante su gestión. La unidad se ha unificado, consolidando la colaboración entre pediatras y mejorando la atención a los pacientes. Actualmente, cuentan con subespecialidades pediátricas completas, excepto oncología, y ofrecen apoyo psicológico a padres de grandes prematuros. La natalidad en Gijón ha descendido notablemente, y la mayoría de los nacimientos actuales provienen de familias inmigrantes. La implementación del Hospital de Día Pediátrico ha permitido atender a cerca de 800 niños al año, liberando camas y mejorando la comodidad de los pacientes. Destaca especialmente el desarrollo de tratamientos para la desensibilización a frutos secos, que proporcionan seguridad a los niños alérgicos y tranquilidad a sus familias. Fernández también expresa preocupación por el aumento de casos de sarampión en España y brotes de meningitis

item.title: Las condiciones críticas en los centros de detención de inmigrantes en EE. UU.
item.description: La noticia describe la situación alarmante en los centros de detención de inmigrantes gestionados por el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas de EE. UU. (ICE). Aunque no son campos de exterminio, las condiciones de hacinamiento, la falta de atención médica adecuada y la negligencia han causado múltiples muertes de detenidos, como Emmanuel Damas y Geraldo Lunas Campos. La mayoría de los internos no han sido juzgados ni condenados por delitos criminales, sino que están detenidos por su estatus migratorio, lo que los sitúa fuera de las protecciones legales normales. La noticia señala que estas instalaciones recuerdan históricamente a otros campos de concentración, incluyendo la práctica de reconcentración española en Cuba y los campos estadounidenses durante la Segunda Guerra Mundial para ciudadanos japoneses. Además, se destaca la implicación de empresas privadas en la gestión de los centros, así como la inversión millonaria de fondos públicos, lo que genera beneficios económicos

item.title: España mantiene precios de electricidad más bajos gracias a renovables y energía nuclear
item.description: España está mostrando una mayor resiliencia frente a la actual crisis energética europea, en gran parte debido a su fuerte apuesta por las energías renovables y la energía nuclear.
